front.title: Recursive Error Correction
front.subtitle: A self-refining process where an AI system iteratively identifies and fixes its own mistakes to improve accuracy.

back.text:
Recursive Error Correction is a computational strategy where a system applies its logic to its own output to detect and rectify inconsistencies. In the context of Large Language Models and neural networks, this involves a multi-pass approach: the model generates a solution, critiques that solution based on predefined constraints, and then regenerates or adjusts the response. This feedback loop continues until the error rate falls below a specific threshold or a maximum number of iterations is reached.

This technique is vital for complex reasoning tasks, such as mathematical problem-solving and software engineering. By treating the initial output as a draft rather than a final product, the system can simulate a form of self-reflection. This reduces hallucinations and logical fallacies, allowing AI to achieve higher precision in high-stakes environments where a single error could lead to system failure.
